title: IICRC S520:2023 Mould Remediation — Assessment, Containment, Remediation & Clearance for Australian Technicians
description: A technical guide to IICRC S520:2023 mould remediation — the four-stage process of assessment, containment, remediation, and post-remediation verification. Essential knowledge for Australian restoration technicians working on water damage and mould claims.
author: Phill McGurk | RestoreAssist
transcript:
## IICRC S520:2023 — The Mould Remediation Standard

IICRC S520:2023 is the primary standard governing mould assessment and remediation in the restoration industry. It defines the roles of the Industrial Hygienist (IH), the Mould Remediation Contractor (MRC), and the project scope — from initial assessment through to final clearance testing.

In Australia, IICRC S520 is referenced by insurers, hygienists, and restoration contractors as the baseline methodology for mould remediation claims. Understanding the four-stage process is essential for any technician working on jobs that involve mould growth or long-term water intrusion.

## Stage 1: Assessment

The assessment phase is typically conducted by a qualified industrial hygienist, not the remediation contractor, to avoid a conflict of interest. The IH will:

- Visually inspect the affected area and identify all areas of visible mould growth
- Collect air samples (spore trap or culture) inside and outside the structure as a baseline
- Collect surface samples (tape lift, swab, or bulk) from affected materials
- Define the scope of remediation in a written remediation protocol

The remediation contractor should not commence work without a written remediation protocol from the IH. On smaller jobs, some insurers allow the restoration contractor to conduct a visual assessment only and proceed under IICRC S520 limited or small-scale mould remediation guidelines.

## Stage 2: Containment

Containment prevents the spread of mould spores to unaffected areas of the building during remediation. IICRC S520:2023 defines four containment levels based on the affected area:

- **Limited containment**: <1 m² of mould growth; plastic sheeting over the work area, negative pressure maintained
- **Full containment**: >1 m² and <10 m²; sealed work area with decontamination chamber, continuous negative air
- **Medium and large scale containment**: >10 m²; engineered containment with HEPA-filtered negative air machines, full decontamination procedures

Air movers must not be operated inside containment without HEPA filtration, as they would aerosolise and spread mould spores.

## Stage 3: Remediation

Remediation involves the physical removal or treatment of mould-affected materials. Key principles under IICRC S520:2023:

- Porous materials (drywall, insulation, carpet) with surface mould growth should generally be removed, not treated in place
- Semi-porous materials (timber framing with surface mould) may be mechanically cleaned and treated with an EPA-registered antimicrobial
- HEPA vacuuming before and after wet cleaning reduces airborne spore counts
- Cleaning agents must be approved for mould remediation use — bleach is not recommended for porous surfaces as it does not penetrate to kill mould hyphae in the substrate

All removed materials must be double-bagged in heavy-duty poly bags, sealed, and disposed of in accordance with local waste regulations.

## Stage 4: Post-Remediation Verification (Clearance)

Post-remediation verification (PRV) is conducted by the IH after the contractor has completed remediation and BEFORE containment is taken down. The IH visually inspects for remaining visible mould, collects air and/or surface samples, and confirms that the remediated area meets the clearance criteria defined in the remediation protocol.

Clearance criteria typically require indoor air sample counts to be at or below outdoor reference levels for the same fungi genera, with no visible mould remaining on remediated surfaces. Only after PRV clearance can containment be removed and the area cleared for reoccupation.
videoScript:
G'day, welcome to RestoreAssist. Today we're going through IICRC S520:2023 — the mould remediation standard that governs how we assess, contain, remediate, and clear mould on a restoration job in Australia.

Mould jobs are increasingly common in Australian restoration work, especially after the run of flood and water damage events over recent years. Doing them correctly protects the occupants, protects you legally, and gets the insurance claim paid.

Let me walk you through the four stages.

Stage one is assessment. On anything more than a minor mould issue, this should be done by a qualified industrial hygienist — not the remediation contractor — to avoid a conflict of interest. The hygienist does a visual inspection, takes air and surface samples, and produces a written remediation protocol. That protocol is your authorisation to proceed. Don't start remediation without it on a significant mould job.

Stage two is containment. The goal is to stop mould spores from spreading to unaffected areas while you work. S520 defines containment levels based on the affected area. Less than 1 square metre, limited containment with plastic sheeting and negative pressure is sufficient. 1 to 10 square metres, you need a sealed work area with a decontamination chamber. Above 10 square metres, you're into engineered containment with HEPA-filtered negative air machines.

One thing to be very careful about: don't run air movers inside containment without HEPA filtration. You'll aerosolise the very spores you're trying to contain.

Stage three is remediation. The key principle: porous materials with mould growth — plasterboard, insulation, carpet — generally need to come out. You can't clean mould out of a porous substrate. Semi-porous materials like timber framing can sometimes be HEPA vacuumed, mechanically cleaned, and treated with an EPA-registered antimicrobial. Note that bleach is not recommended by IICRC S520 for surface-only treatment of porous materials — it doesn't penetrate far enough to kill the hyphae in the substrate.

All removed materials go into double heavy-duty poly bags, sealed, and disposed of in accordance with your local regulations.

Stage four is post-remediation verification — PRV. This is the clearance inspection conducted by the hygienist after remediation but before containment comes down. The hygienist checks for visible mould and collects final air samples. Clearance criteria typically require indoor air counts to be at or below outdoor reference levels. Only after PRV sign-off can the area be cleared for reoccupation.

RestoreAssist's inspection workflow includes mould remediation scope templates aligned to IICRC S520, with containment level guidance based on the affected area you enter.

Head to restoreassist.app to explore it.
